pathophysiology_summary: External haemorrhoids are dilated vascular cushions distal to the dentate line, provoked by straining/constipation, producing a soft anal-verge lump, perianal itch and outlet-type bright-red bleeding on wiping. They are benign and respond to fibre/fluid, toileting behaviour and short topical therapy. The critical ground-truth task is that rectal bleeding must never be attributed to haemorrhoids without screening colorectal alarm features (blood mixed in stool, altered bowel habit, weight loss, anaemia, family history, melaena) and arranging age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening; and distinguishing uncomplicated haemorrhoids from a thrombosed external haemorrhoid (tense, hard, dark-purple, excruciating — may need incision within 48–72h) and from heavier lower-GI bleeding. Anoscopy/DRE are in-person; telehealth advises conservative management with a malignancy safety-net and recommends examination.

differential.evidence_basis: Soft anal-verge lump with itch and outlet bright-red bleeding after straining.
PR bleeding mandates screening alarm features and arranging bowel-cancer screening.
Tense hard excruciating dark-purple lump would suggest thrombosis; not the pattern here.
Severe tearing pain on defecation would suggest a fissure; less prominent here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Haemorrhoid may lead, but colorectal malignancy alarm features must be screened and thrombosis excluded before conservative management.

differential.evidence_basis: Outlet bright-red bleeding, soft mildly tender lump, no alarm features, not thrombosed/heavy — uncomplicated external haemorrhoid.
No alarm features; arrange age-appropriate screening and in-person exam — retained as a screening/safety-net.
Not tense/hard/excruciating; escalation target if it thromboses.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative alarm/thrombosis screen, an uncomplicated haemorrhoid leads; fibre + topical + toileting behaviour, with a malignancy screening safety-net and in-person exam recommended.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; benign; confirm with in-person exam and arrange bowel-cancer screening if due.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is a benign external haemorrhoid with a malignancy safety-net.

red_flags.description: Colorectal alarm features — blood mixed in/darker stool or melaena, persistent change in bowel habit, thinner stools, weight loss, anaemia, family history of bowel cancer
red_flags.clinical_significance: PR bleeding can be the presentation of colorectal cancer; alarm features mandate colonoscopy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed cancer diagnosis by attributing all bleeding to haemorrhoids.

red_flags.description: Thrombosed haemorrhoid / heavy bleeding — tense hard excruciating dark-purple lump, or bleeding dripping/pouring into the bowl, or fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Thrombosis may need incision within 48–72h; heavy bleeding needs assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated painful thrombosis / significant blood loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attributing all PR bleeding to haemorrhoids
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening colorectal alarm features / not arranging screening — the classic dangerous shortcut.
Not asking about a tense, hard, excruciating lump amenable to early incision.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Perianal inspection, digital rectal examination and anoscopy
Assessment of the lump's thrombosis and internal haemorrhoids/masses
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Fibre/fluid + topical + toileting behaviour with a recommendation for in-person examination and age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening; ED for heavy bleeding/severe thrombosis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Conservative management: increase dietary fibre and fluids, avoid straining, short-course topical preparations for symptoms. Examine and investigate rectal bleeding appropriately; do not attribute bleeding to haemorrhoids without excluding other causes / arranging screening.
